figures.caption: Figure 6: Example of an E2W-2 sample (Case 2). The model receives two views of a table scene and must identify the third item from the left by integrating spatial information across views.
Table 4: Left: Where2Place results. Right: Real-world robot evaluation.

Figure 1: An illustration of collaborative spatial reasoning in embodied systems. Reasoning from a single viewpoint fails due to occlusions or a limited field of view. In contrast, cross-view compositional reasoning integrates multiple perspectives to correctly localize and grasp the target object—the blue block f rthest from the strawberry.
Table 1: Performance on E2W-Bench. Tasks are grouped into Reasoning (accuracy, %) and Perception (score, %). (S) = simulation, (R) = real-world. Best in bold, second best underlined.

Figure 7: Example of an E2W-3 sample. The model receives two ego-centric views from different robot perspectives and must localize the target object—in this case, the pizza—and output the precise grasp location. Solving the task requires cross-view object correspondence, reasoning about spatial ordering, identifying the visible target in each view, and determining the final grasp coordinate in the main perspective.

Figure 5: Example of an E2W-1 sample (Case 1). Two ego-centric views of a kitchen scene are provided, and the model must count the total number of pizzas by resolving cross-view object correspondences.

Figure 3: CoRL framework. The model is first initialized via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on Chain-of-Thought annotations, then refined with reinforcement learning (RL). During RL, the policy is optimized with an format reward and the Cross-View Spatial Reward (CVSR), which supplies dense feedback on cross-view fusion and spatial consistency, guiding robust collaborative reasoning.
